title: The Coca-Cola Company (KO) Intrinsic Value
description: An independent discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of The Coca-Cola Company (KO) compiled entirely by Gemini 3.1.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale: 2.5% matches global GDP expectations and long-term inflation targets. Coca-Cola is the quintessential defensive stock; as long as inflation exists, Coca-Cola will raise prices to match it in perpetuity.

thesisTitle: The AI Thesis: Why I'm Modelling Steady, Reliable Growth
thesisParagraphs: The Coca-Cola Company (KO) isn't an explosive tech growth story. It's a consumer staples juggernaut built on an unparalleled global distribution network, immense pricing power, and brand equity that spans generations. While volume growth in North America remains sluggish, Coca-Cola consistently offsets this through strategic pricing actions—proving it is one of the market's greatest inflation hedges.

My valuation model focuses heavily on Coca-Cola's transition to a more asset-light model via bottling refranchising. By shedding lower-margin capital-intensive bottling operations, the company has structurally improved operating margins and free cash flow conversion. The $5.29B FCF base (FY25) is poised for reliable, steady compounding.

risks.introText: While my valuation expects stable compounding, there are structural headwinds that could challenge these assumptions:

items.title: 1. GLP-1 and Shifting Consumer Habits
items.description: The rise of GLP-1 weight-loss drugs and a broader societal shift away from sugary beverages pose long-term volume risks. While KO has successfully pivoted to zero-sugar, water, and coffee segments, its core sugared sodas still command significant margin share.

items.title: 2. Currency Headwinds
items.description: Because Coca-Cola is a global behemoth generating the majority of its revenue outside the US, a strong US dollar severely compresses reported earnings and cash flows when foreign profits are repatriated.

items.title: 3. Debt Burden and Rates
items.description: With roughly $45B in total debt compared to $15.8B in cash/short-term investments, Coca-Cola carries significant leverage. In a persistently high-interest-rate environment, refinancing this debt will increase interest expense, acting as a drag on future Free Cash Flow.

verdictFooter: Based on FY2025 actuals. Data via S&P Global (Mar 18, 2026).

faq.question: Why did Gemini pick a 5.5% growth rate for Coca-Cola?
faq.answer: Gemini projects 5.5% FCF growth based on Coca-Cola's unparalleled pricing power, dominant global brand equity, and ongoing margin expansion driven by its asset-light franchising model.

faq.question: What discount rate was used for Coca-Cola's DCF?
faq.answer: A 7.5% discount rate was selected. This low WACC acknowledges Coca-Cola's extremely stable cash flows, beta near 0.60, and resilience during economic downturns, offset slightly by its significant debt load.

faq.question: Why is the calculated Intrinsic Value lower than the current stock price?
faq.answer: Consumer staples giants like Coca-Cola often trade at a premium to standard DCF models due to their perceived safety, consistent dividend payouts, and low volatility. The market is willing to pay a higher multiple for earnings certainty.
